### Market Efficiency and Its Limits at Tick Frequency

The anomaly literature — January effects, size premiums, momentum, PEAD — is
built on daily and monthly return data. These patterns operate at timescales
that are structurally incompatible with the trading problem studied in this
thesis. AAPL is a large-cap, heavily traded equity with sub-millisecond order
arrival rates; the behavioral biases and institutional frictions that sustain
calendar anomalies or the value premium are largely irrelevant at tick
frequency. The relevant question at the order-book level is not whether
prices fully reflect publicly available fundamental information, but whether
the mechanics of order-book dynamics produce short-lived, exploitable
structure before it is arbitraged away.

This distinction is not a technicality. @Fama1970 explicitly noted that the
efficient markets hypothesis is a statement about information incorporation
speed relative to trading frictions — a claim calibrated to the market
microstructure of its era. At sub-second resolution, the relevant frictions
are adverse selection, queue priority, and latency rather than analyst
coverage or earnings processing delays. The question of market efficiency
therefore reads differently at the microstructure level.

**Short-term predictability from order flow.** The most directly relevant
body of evidence is the empirical literature showing that order-book
quantities predict near-term price changes at horizons of seconds to minutes.
@Cont2014 demonstrate that order flow imbalance (OFI) — the net directional
pressure from order submissions and cancellations — explains a substantial
fraction of contemporaneous and lagged price movements, with $R^2$ values
reaching 65% in their sample. @Stoikov2018 shows that the microprice, a
volume-weighted estimate of where the next transaction will occur, is a
better predictor of future mid-price than the mid-price itself. These are not
calendar-effect-style anomalies that can be slowly arbitraged; they are
structural features of how prices are formed in a continuous double auction.

**Adverse selection as a recurring regularity.** The Glosten-Milgrom
framework (@GlostenMilgrom1985; @Kyle1985) implies that spreads and depth
are endogenous responses to the presence of informed traders. This creates a
persistent regularity: at times of elevated adverse selection risk, spreads
widen, depth thins, and price impact per unit of volume increases. These
transitions are predictable — not in the sense of knowing which direction
prices will move, but in the sense that the cost of trading is elevated and
the probability of being adversely selected is higher. An agent that
conditions its position on the current adverse selection regime can reduce
execution costs and improve risk-adjusted returns simply by knowing when
_not_ to trade aggressively. The spread ratio and OFI features used in this
thesis are direct operationalizations of this logic.

**Intraday seasonality.** @Wood1985 and @Harris1986 document a robust
U-shaped intraday pattern in both volume and spread: elevated activity and
wider spreads at the open and close, compressed costs and lower turnover in
mid-session. This regularity reflects the concentration of informed trading
at session boundaries and has been stable for decades. It constitutes a
predictable, theoretically grounded regularity at intraday frequency — not
the kind of anomaly that funds will arbitrage away, because it is driven by
structural demand for liquidity at open and close rather than by
mispricing. The hour-of-day cyclical encodings in the feature set capture
this pattern directly.

**Limits to arbitrage at HFT timescales.** @Shleifer1997 identify three
conditions that allow anomalies to persist: noise trader risk, synchronization
risk, and transaction costs. At tick frequency, analogous frictions apply but
in a different form. Queue position risk — the uncertainty about fill
probabilities and execution quality when resting in the order book — creates
a structural barrier to pure arbitrage even when a price signal is strong.
Latency means that identified mispricings may resolve before an order reaches
the exchange. These constraints imply that microstructure regularities can
persist not because of behavioral biases, but because they are difficult to
exploit fully even when they are known to exist.

The implication for this thesis is that the relevant "exploitable structure"
is not a calendar effect or a factor premium. It is the short-lived
information content of order-book dynamics — OFI, queue depletion, spread
regime, microprice divergence — which literature has documented to be
real, theoretically grounded, and persistent at the timescales at which the
agent operates. The challenge is not identifying these patterns but learning
when they are currently informative and how to size positions accordingly
under continuous transaction costs.
